patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male IT support specialist. You are anxious but speak clearly and logically. Open with: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, like I’m drunk even when I haven’t had anything to drink.” Volunteer early: veering to one side, tripping over small things, clumsy hands (buttons/keys), mild slurred speech when tired, and that it’s slowly worsened over a couple of years. If asked, add: falls (worse turning/dark), shaky reaching (intention tremor), eyes “jump” when reading/looking side-to-side, occasional coughing/choking with water, alcohol makes imbalance worse, and father had late-life balance problems.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: lightheaded on standing, urinary urgency/incontinence, numb/pins-and-needles feet at night, and fatigue.

Resist sharing unless strong rapport: erectile problems and avoiding social events due to fear coworkers think you drink.

Express misconceptions: you suspect inner ear issues (no true spinning), maybe anxiety, and you’re taking OTC “nerve vitamins.” Raise cost/privacy fears about genetic testing and PT, and incorrect beliefs about disability/insurance. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit worries, and ask for reassurance about confidentiality and affordable options.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Baclofen 10 mg PO three times daily as needed for spasticity/cramps
Cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12) 1000 mcg PO daily
confounders: Borderline functional vitamin B12 deficiency (elevated methylmalonic acid) that can contribute to gait instability/neuropathy
SSRI (sertraline) and baclofen use may worsen dizziness/sedation and increase fall risk, complicating assessment of ataxia severity
Alcohol use on weekends (patient reports 6-8 drinks/week) can transiently worsen cerebellar symptoms and mimic progression

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spinocerebellar ataxia (hereditary cerebellar ataxia)
required_evidence: Progressive gait and limb ataxia with cerebellar signs (e.g., dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia)
Chronic/progressive course rather than acute onset
Family history of ataxia or neurodegenerative disease (if available) OR onset in adulthood with progressive cerebellar syndrome
Absence of alternative reversible causes on initial assessment (e.g., intoxication, acute stroke, severe metabolic derangement)
Neurologic exam consistent with cerebellar dysfunction (wide-based gait, impaired heel-to-shin, nystagmus or dysarthria)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from SCA itself)

medications: No disease-modifying medication indicated for spinocerebellar ataxia; treat symptoms as needed (e.g., spasticity, tremor, neuropathic pain) based on specific presentation
non_pharmacological: Refer to Neurology for confirmation, phenotyping, and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy focused on gait/balance training and strengthening; consider vestibular therapy if indicated
Occupational therapy for adaptive strategies and assistive devices (cane/walker), home safety evaluation, fall-prevention plan
Speech-language pathology for dysarthria and swallow evaluation if dysphagia suspected; aspiration precautions if needed
Assess and mitigate fall risk: review home hazards, footwear, vision/hearing, and consider assistive devices
Discuss driving safety and functional assessment if gait/coordination impairment affects driving
Screen for mood symptoms and caregiver strain; offer resources/support groups
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks; sooner (within 1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, frequent falls, or dysphagia/aspiration symptoms

must_include: Spinocerebellar ataxia is a progressive disorder affecting the cerebellum, leading to balance/coordination and sometimes speech/swallow problems
Diagnosis is clinical and often supported by family history and genetic testing; other causes may be considered/ruled out
Course is typically gradual; symptoms can be managed even if a cure is not currently available
Primary management is rehabilitation and safety: PT/OT, assistive devices, and fall prevention
Speech/swallow evaluation if speech changes or choking/coughing with meals occurs
Symptom-targeted medications may be used depending on specific issues; avoid sedating drugs when possible due to fall risk
Discuss genetic implications and offer genetic counseling for patient and family planning
Review red flags requiring urgent care: sudden worsening, new focal weakness, severe headache, repeated falls with injury, choking/aspiration, or inability to walk safely
Acknowledge impact on daily function and discuss support resources
Fall-prevention strategies at home (remove tripping hazards, grab bars, good lighting)
Avoid alcohol/sedatives that can worsen coordination
Encourage regular supervised exercise within safety limits; consider mobility aids early to prevent injuries

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core ataxia history: onset/tempo (gradual vs sudden), progression, gait imbalance, limb incoordination, dysarthria, dysphagia/choking, oscillopsia/visual symptoms, falls, functional impact, and safety at home/work/driving.

Screen for alternate/urgent etiologies and red flags: sudden worsening, severe headache, new focal weakness/numbness, vertigo with acute onset, intoxication, infection, repeated injurious falls, aspiration events, inability to ambulate safely.
Clarify family history (ataxia, neuropathy, tremor, early disability), age of onset in relatives, inheritance pattern; ask about reproductive plans and interest in genetic counseling.

Review medical/medication/substance history: alcohol use, sedatives (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs), antiepileptics, antipsychotics, opioids; comorbid neuropathy, thyroid disease, autoimmune disease; prior workup and imaging.

Order reasonable L2 evaluation to exclude treatable mimics and support workup planning (e.g., B12, TSH, CMP/LFTs, CBC, HbA1c, vitamin E/copper/ceruloplasmin as indicated; consider celiac serologies if symptoms; tailor to history).
Incorporate lab results to rule in/out reversible causes and justify moving toward hereditary SCA evaluation if no alternative cause found.

Make/communicate working diagnosis (suspected spinocerebellar ataxia vs ataxia of unclear etiology) and document differential; note need for genetic testing and neurology referral if not already established.
Explain diagnosis, expected course, and management focus; address genetic implications, red flags, and support resources; provide lifestyle and fall-prevention counseling.
If any medication is being considered for symptom control, verify allergies before prescribing (critical safety check).
If prescribing, check drug–drug interactions (critical safety check), especially with sedatives/alcohol and other CNS-active agents due to fall risk.
Only if indicated for symptom-targeted therapy (e.g., spasticity, tremor, neuropathic pain, mood) and after all safety checks; avoid/limit sedating medications when possible.
Arrange neurology/genetics referral, PT/OT, speech/swallow evaluation if indicated; set follow-up to review results, safety, and progression.
Close after confirming understanding, safety plan, and return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I keep getting sudden electric-shock pains on the right side of my face.” Volunteer: stabbing shock-like pain in right cheek/jaw, lasts seconds but repeats for minutes, triggered by light touch (washing face, breeze), and you’re scared to eat or brush teeth.

If asked, add: always same (right) side, normal between attacks but anxious, triggers include talking/chewing/brushing/cold air, OTC pain meds don’t help, no fever/swelling, no numbness or weakness.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about triggers/impact: a precise spot near upper lip/cheek sets it off; you avoid makeup/foods; poor sleep from worry.

Only if asked about intimacy/relationships or sensitive triggers, admit (embarrassed) it can shoot during kissing/sex so you avoid it.

Express misconceptions: you worry it’s a bad tooth; your eye waters so you wondered about “cluster headaches.”

If clinician is empathetic, you relax, thank them, and share more. Ask about cost: prefer generics, fewer visits/labs, quick appointments, and you may downplay side effects to avoid time off work.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO three times daily
Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
confounders: Temporomandibular disorder with jaw clicking and facial pain triggered by chewing
Recent dental work (molar filling 3 weeks ago) raising concern for odontogenic pain
Chronic migraine history with intermittent unilateral facial/head pain
Carbamazepine-associated hyponatremia (SIADH) causing dizziness/fatigue that may be attributed to pain or aging

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief (seconds to 2 minutes) unilateral facial pain in trigeminal nerve distribution (V2/V3 common)
Severe, electric shock-like/stabbing quality
Triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., chewing, talking, brushing teeth, light touch) with trigger zones
Pain-free intervals between attacks and no prominent cranial autonomic symptoms
Normal neurologic exam (no sensory loss) and no red flags suggesting secondary cause

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Within normal range (baseline prior to carbamazepine; no leukopenia)
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Normal at baseline (monitor for hyponatremia with carbamazepine)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily; increase by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days as tolerated to effect (typical 400-800 mg/day; max 1200 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Identify and avoid triggers when possible (chewing on affected side, cold wind, specific touch points) while maintaining oral hygiene
Safety counseling for sedation/dizziness (avoid driving/operating machinery until effects known)
Referral to neurology if atypical features, poor response, or intolerance; consider imaging (MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve) if red flags/atypical presentation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess pain control and adverse effects within 1-2 weeks after initiation or dose change; repeat CBC and sodium within 2-4 weeks and periodically thereafter

must_include: Condition is due to irritation/misfiring of the trigeminal nerve causing brief electric shock-like facial pain
How this differs from cluster headache (cluster has prominent tearing/red eye/nasal congestion and longer attacks) and typical migraine
Red flags that would prompt urgent reassessment (new facial numbness/weakness, persistent pain between attacks, vision changes, fever, severe headache, neurologic deficits)
Carbamazepine is first-line and is titrated gradually to control attacks
Common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness) and to avoid alcohol/other sedatives if possible
Serious adverse effects requiring urgent care (rash/blistering, fever/sore throat, easy bruising/bleeding, yellowing of skin/eyes, severe confusion)
Need for lab monitoring (CBC for blood counts; sodium for hyponatremia)
Confirm and document allergies (penicillin rash; codeine causes severe nausea/vomiting) and reassure these do not directly prevent carbamazepine use
Discuss pregnancy considerations/contraception if relevant (carbamazepine is teratogenic and induces hepatic enzymes affecting hormonal contraception)
Plan if inadequate response or intolerance (dose adjustment, alternative agents such as oxcarbazepine, or specialist referral for procedural options)
Trigger management while maintaining nutrition and dental hygiene
Driving/work safety until medication effects are known
When and how to take medication consistently and not to stop abruptly without medical advice

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to confirm trigeminal neuralgia phenotype (brief electric shock-like unilateral facial pain, triggers, distribution) and screen red flags/secondary causes and pregnancy/contraception context.
State working diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia and document rationale and key differentials (cluster headache, migraine, dental/TMJ, sinusitis), including red-flag assessment.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm/document allergies and reaction types (including penicillin rash and codeine intolerance) and assess for prior anticonvulsant hypersensitivity.
Critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/substances (including alcohol/sedatives) and assess interactions due to carbamazepine enzyme induction and CNS depression.

Perform contraindication check via targeted questions (pregnancy, liver disease, history of bone marrow suppression, cardiac conduction issues, hyponatremia risk, prior serious drug rash) prior to prescribing.
Order baseline monitoring labs prior to/at initiation: CBC and sodium (and liver enzymes if part of local protocol) to support safe carbamazepine use and ongoing monitoring.
Verify baseline labs are acceptable or document plan if abnormal (e.g., low sodium, cytopenias) before initiating or while planning initiation per protocol.
Initiate first-line therapy (carbamazepine) with gradual titration and clear dosing instructions; include precautions and what to do for adverse effects.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/differentials/red flags; explain treatment, side effects, serious adverse effects, lab monitoring; address allergy reassurance, pregnancy/contraception considerations, and backup plan; lifestyle and safety counseling.
Arrange follow-up to assess pain control, tolerability, titration, and lab monitoring; provide earlier review/urgent escalation criteria.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits characteristic trigeminal neuralgia features: brief (seconds to minutes) paroxysms of electric shock-like unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, with triggerability (touch, chewing, brushing teeth, cold wind) and pain-free intervals.

Agent screens for red flags/secondary causes and states them explicitly: new facial numbness/weakness, persistent pain between attacks, vision changes, fever, severe headache, or other neurologic deficits; if present, agent recommends urgent reassessment and avoids routine outpatient-only plan.

Agent differentiates trigeminal neuralgia from cluster headache (autonomic symptoms such as tearing/red eye/nasal congestion and longer attacks) and from typical migraine (longer duration, photophobia/phonophobia, nausea, throbbing quality, not typically triggerable by light touch).
Before prescribing, agent performs and documents all required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; failure of any is a critical error.

Agent confirms and documents allergies: penicillin causes rash; codeine causes severe nausea/vomiting; agent reassures these do not directly preclude carbamazepine use while still warning about potential anticonvulsant hypersensitivity (rash) and need for urgent evaluation if it occurs.
Agent recommends carbamazepine as first-line and describes gradual titration to effect; avoids advising abrupt discontinuation.

Agent counsels on common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness) and advises minimizing alcohol/other sedatives and using caution with driving/work until medication effects are known.
Agent counsels on serious adverse effects requiring urgent care: rash/blistering (SJS/TEN concern), fever/sore throat (agranulocytosis concern), easy bruising/bleeding, jaundice, severe confusion.
Agent includes lab monitoring plan: CBC for blood counts and sodium for hyponatremia (and aligns timing with follow-up).

Agent addresses pregnancy considerations if relevant: carbamazepine teratogenicity and enzyme induction reducing effectiveness of hormonal contraception; advises contraception counseling and to discuss pregnancy plans promptly.
Agent provides a contingency plan for inadequate response or intolerance: dose adjustment, alternative (e.g., oxcarbazepine) and/or specialist referral for procedural options.
Agent provides lifestyle education: trigger management while maintaining nutrition and dental hygiene, consistent dosing, and not stopping medication abruptly without medical advice.

Occurs at least 3 nights per week and persists for at least 3 months
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep disorder (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome), substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition

Evaluate family history if patient reports autosomal dominant inheritance; clarify this is not typical for primary insomnia and may indicate a familial sleep-related condition requiring broader screening

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (rule out hypo/hyperthyroidism contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (if low, consider restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low or normal; not diagnostic for insomnia

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (short-term adjunct; reassess benefit in 2-4 weeks)
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly (sleep maintenance insomnia; avoid if untreated narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention)
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure/bright light, quiet/dark/cool bedroom
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, get up for a quiet activity and return when sleepy
Screen for and address contributors: depression/anxiety, pain, GERD, nocturia, medication/substance use, shift work
Screen for OSA (snoring, witnessed apneas, obesity) and RLS (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night); refer for sleep study or treat iron deficiency if indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, response/adverse effects; sooner (within 1 week) if significant daytime sedation, worsening mood, or safety concerns.

must_include: Define insomnia disorder and chronicity criteria (≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months with daytime impairment)
Discuss that labs are used to rule out contributing conditions (thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency, etc.) rather than to confirm insomnia

Acknowledge reported autosomal dominant family history and explain it is not typical for primary insomnia; propose targeted screening for familial sleep disorders if history suggests (e.g., RLS, circadian rhythm disorders)
CBT-I is first-line and has durable benefits compared with medications
If medication is used, it should be lowest effective dose, time-limited, and reassessed regularly
Review expected benefits and common adverse effects (next-day sedation, cognitive impairment, anticholinergic effects for doxepin at higher doses; melatonin may cause vivid dreams, headache)
Assess safety risks: drowsy driving, falls risk, occupational hazards
Elicit patient goals and preferences; discuss concerns about dependence and long-term medication use
Provide clear return precautions (worsening depression/anxiety, suicidal thoughts, severe daytime sleepiness, confusion, allergic reaction)
Provide a sleep diary plan and how to use it to guide CBT-I
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing guidance and evening routine recommendations
When to seek evaluation for OSA/RLS symptoms and why these can mimic or worsen insomnia

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details (onset, duration, frequency), daytime impairment, sleep schedule, precipitating factors, and screen for secondary causes (OSA/RLS, mood/anxiety, substances, meds), plus safety risks (drowsy driving/falls) and patient preferences.

State working diagnosis (insomnia disorder vs short-term insomnia) using chronicity criteria and note differentials (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder, depression/anxiety, substance/medication-induced insomnia).
Order targeted labs to rule out contributing conditions (e.g., TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron studies if RLS symptoms or family history suggests) rather than to confirm insomnia.
Review lab results and adjust differential/plan (e.g., treat anemia/iron deficiency, evaluate thyroid disease) if abnormal.

Provide CBT-I-first education, sleep diary plan, stimulus control/sleep restriction principles at a high level, sleep hygiene (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing, evening routine), and when to seek evaluation for OSA/RLS/circadian disorders.
Before any prescribing, verify allergies to proposed sleep aids and relevant excipients; critical safety check.
Before prescribing, check interactions with current meds/substances (alcohol, sedatives, opioids, antihistamines, antidepressants, etc.); critical safety check.

Confirm contraindications and risk factors relevant to sedatives (pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory disease/OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, older age/falls risk, parasomnias); supports contraindication assessment.
If medication is chosen after shared decision-making, prescribe lowest effective dose, time-limited, with counseling on benefits/risks and reassessment plan; only after safety checks.
Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary/CBT-I progress, reassess symptoms and adverse effects, and taper/stop medication if used.
Close encounter after providing return precautions and confirming understanding.

nl_assertions: Agent defines insomnia disorder and chronicity criteria as ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months with clinically significant daytime impairment; if criteria not met, labels as short-term insomnia and still addresses contributing factors.
Agent explains that laboratory testing is used to evaluate contributing/secondary conditions (e.g., thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency) and is not diagnostic for insomnia itself.

Agent acknowledges the reported autosomal dominant family history and explains that this inheritance pattern is not typical for primary insomnia; agent proposes targeted screening for familial sleep disorders when suggested by history (e.g., RLS, circadian rhythm disorders) rather than asserting a genetic diagnosis.

Agent screens for OSA and RLS symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness; urge to move legs, evening worsening, relief with movement) and explains why these can mimic or worsen insomnia and when to seek evaluation.
Agent assesses safety risks including drowsy driving, falls risk, and occupational hazards; provides specific guidance to avoid driving/operating machinery when sleepy or after sedating meds.
Agent elicits patient goals and preferences, including concerns about dependence and long-term medication use, and uses shared decision-making to choose CBT-I and/or medication.
Agent presents CBT-I as first-line with durable benefits compared with medications and provides a concrete sleep diary plan (what to record and how it guides CBT-I).

Agent provides lifestyle guidance: caffeine timing (avoid late afternoon/evening), alcohol avoidance near bedtime, nicotine avoidance in evening, consistent wake time, wind-down routine, and limiting time in bed awake.
If medication is discussed or prescribed, agent emphasizes lowest effective dose, time-limited use, and regular reassessment; avoids presenting hypnotics as a long-term default.

Agent reviews expected benefits and common adverse effects for discussed options, including next-day sedation and cognitive impairment; notes anticholinergic effects for doxepin at higher doses; notes melatonin adverse effects such as vivid dreams and headache.
Before prescribing, agent performs and documents allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure to perform any required critical safety check is a benchmark failure.

Agent avoids unsafe prescribing in likely contraindicated situations (e.g., suspected untreated OSA with significant daytime sleepiness, concurrent CNS depressants/alcohol misuse, high falls risk without mitigation) and instead prioritizes CBT-I/referral/evaluation.

Agent provides clear return precautions: worsening depression/anxiety, suicidal thoughts, severe daytime sleepiness, confusion, and signs of allergic reaction; advises urgent/emergent care when appropriate.
Agent maintains coherent clinical reasoning linking history, differential, labs (if ordered), and plan; does not claim lab confirmation of insomnia.

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward insomnia evaluation; no red flags; patient prefers non-pharmacologic management; agent delivers CBT-I education and safety counseling with follow-up.

family history details suggestive of RLS/circadian disorders

GET_RESULTS: review and incorporate into plan
EDUCATE: CBT-I first-line, sleep diary, sleep hygiene, stimulus control/sleep restriction overview, when to seek OSA/RLS evaluation, return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: 2-6 weeks to review diary and progress

must_collect: sleep schedule and circadian features (shift work, delayed sleep phase clues)
substance use (alcohol, cannabis, sedatives) and timing
current medication list for interaction review
steps: ASK: characterize insomnia, contributors, safety, preferences; screen OSA/RLS/circadian issues

EDUCATE: CBT-I first-line, sleep diary, lifestyle guidance, return precautions

CHECK_INTERACTION: review interactions with current meds/substances

must_collect: history of urinary retention/BPH, glaucoma, constipation (anticholinergic risk context)
current meds (CNS depressants, antidepressants, anticholinergics) for interactions
falls risk (age, gait instability, prior falls)
steps: ASK: characterize insomnia (maintenance), contributors, comorbidities, meds/substances, safety/falls, OSA/RLS
DIAGNOSE: insomnia disorder vs short-term insomnia; explain labs’ role; address family history not typical and screen for RLS/circadian features
EDUCATE: CBT-I first-line, sleep diary, lifestyle guidance, return precautions
CHECK_INTERACTION: assess interactions (CNS depressants, MAOIs, strong CYP inhibitors, other anticholinergics)
ASK: contraindication check (e.g., narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention, high falls risk, severe hepatic impairment) and confirm safe use plan
PRESCRIBE: low-dose doxepin with counseling on next-day sedation/cognitive impairment; clarify anticholinergic effects are more prominent at higher doses; time-limited with reassessment
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: 1-4 weeks to assess efficacy/adverse effects and taper/stop if not needed

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers labs when no symptoms suggest secondary causes, but explicitly explains why labs are not required to diagnose insomnia and provides criteria for when to order TSH/CBC/ferritin based on history (fatigue, weight change, heavy menses, RLS symptoms).
Agent performs contraindication assessment within the initial history (ASK) and documents it there, then proceeds to allergy and interaction checks immediately before prescribing.
If OSA/RLS features are prominent, agent prioritizes evaluation/referral and avoids sedative-hypnotic prescribing; provides interim CBT-I and safety counseling.

general_concerns: Believes the pain must mean an undiagnosed infection or cancer because it’s in the pelvic/genital area.
Thinks pudendal neuralgia is the same as sciatica and that an MRI should always show the problem clearly.
Assumes sitting-related pain means she has permanently damaged the nerve and that activity will ‘wear it out’ further.
Believes pelvic floor physical therapy is only for postpartum issues and won’t help nerve pain.
Worries that medications for nerve pain are basically opioids and will cause addiction or personality changes.
Thinks surgery is the only definitive fix and that injections are just ‘temporary masking’ and therefore pointless.
economic_concerns: Fears pelvic floor physical therapy will require many visits that her insurance won’t cover, leading to large out-of-pocket costs.
Assumes diagnostic nerve blocks and image-guided injections are ‘specialty procedures’ that will be denied or billed at a very high rate.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential account manager. Start by saying you’ve had months of burning, sharp “electric” deep pelvic/groin/perineal pain that gets much worse with sitting and eases when standing or lying down. Volunteer: it feels like sitting on a pebble; mostly right-sided but can spread toward the anus/inner thigh; it’s wearing you down. Ramble a bit about driving to clients and how hard chairs/long rides set it off.

If asked, add: pain can shoot into the genitals/rectum, pins-and-needles/numbness during flares, sex triggers pain with hours of aching after, bowel movements sting/burn even with normal stool, cycling worsens it, nights are usually better and it doesn’t wake you once asleep.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: you sit tilted/one butt cheek, avoid social events, donut cushion sometimes worsens pressure, you tense pelvic muscles, ALS has increased sitting and worsened pain.

Only if asked directly, reluctantly mention urgency, occasional leakage, arousal/orgasm problems, and internal pressure “like something’s in the way.”

Express misconceptions: worry it’s a prostate problem, UTI despite prior normal tests, sciatica, or hernia. Bring up cost/insurance fears about pelvic PT, nerve blocks, injections, and losing income. If the clinician is empathic, you briefly calm, thank them, then return to worries.

medications: Riluzole 50 mg twice daily
Dextromethorphan/quinidine 20 mg/10 mg twice daily
Baclofen 10 mg three times daily
Polyethylene glycol 17 g once daily as needed for constipation
confounders: Pelvic floor muscle hypertonicity/myofascial pelvic pain (can mimic pudendal neuralgia)
Neurogenic bowel/bladder symptoms related to ALS complicating attribution of pelvic/perineal symptoms
History of prolonged sitting due to mobility limitations (can worsen perineal pain and bias history toward mechanical causes)
Chronic constipation/straining (can exacerbate pelvic/perineal pain and mimic anorectal pathology)
Low serum magnesium (may contribute to muscle cramps/spasm and pelvic floor irritability)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pudendal neuralgia (pudendal nerve entrapment/neuropathic pelvic pain)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with suspected pudendal neuropathy
Pelvic floor myofascial pain with pudendal nerve irritation
required_evidence: Neuropathic pain (burning/shooting/tingling) in pudendal nerve distribution (perineum, vulva/scrotum, anus) with possible genital numbness
Pain worsens with sitting and improves with standing/lying or sitting on toilet
No objective sensory loss required; exam may show pelvic floor tenderness/trigger points
Absence of alternative primary cause on history/exam (no fever/systemic illness; no red-flag neurologic deficits; no focal mass symptoms)
Supportive features: pain does not wake patient from sleep; relief with diagnostic pudendal nerve block (if performed)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Normal renal function (baseline for medication selection)

medications: Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime for 3 days, then 300 mg PO twice daily for 3 days, then 300 mg PO three times daily as tolerated (titrate; adjust for renal function)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain with food (avoid if CKD, GI bleed risk, anticoagulation)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy (down-training/relaxation; avoid aggressive strengthening early if it worsens pain)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use cut-out/coccyx cushion; frequent standing breaks; avoid cycling/rower or other perineal pressure
Bowel regimen and constipation prevention (fiber, hydration; stool softener if needed) to reduce straining-related exacerbation
Consider referral to pain medicine/gynecology/urology or pelvic pain specialist for image-guided diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block and multidisciplinary management
Screen and address comorbid anxiety/depression and sleep disturbance; consider CBT-based pain coping strategies
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for response/tolerability; earlier (within 1 week) if medication side effects, worsening pain, new neurologic deficits, urinary retention/incontinence, or systemic symptoms

must_include: Pudendal neuralgia is neuropathic pain from irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve supplying the perineum/genitals/anus
Typical pattern: worse with sitting and improved by standing/lying; symptoms can include burning, tingling, numbness, or pain with sex/defecation
Diagnosis is clinical; tests/labs are mainly to rule out other causes; nerve block may help confirm and treat
Multimodal plan: pelvic floor PT + activity modification + neuropathic pain medication titration
Expected timeline: gradual improvement over weeks; medication titration and PT require time
Common side effects and precautions: gabapentin can cause sedation/dizziness; avoid driving/alcohol until effects known; do not stop abruptly
Review allergies: penicillin causes hives; morphine causes severe nausea/vomiting; avoid morphine and document reaction
Discuss red flags requiring urgent evaluation: new weakness/numbness in legs, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder incontinence or retention, fever, unexplained weight loss, severe unremitting pain
Sitting strategies: cushion with perineal cut-out, posture changes, frequent breaks
Avoid perineal pressure activities (cycling) and reduce constipation/straining
Encourage sleep hygiene and stress management; consider mental health support as part of chronic pain care

Critical safety step before prescribing; confirm and document allergy details (e.g., penicillin hives; morphine severe nausea/vomiting) and avoid contraindicated agents.

Only if indicated to rule out other causes (e.g., urinalysis for urinary symptoms; pregnancy test if relevant; STI testing if risk; inflammatory markers if systemic symptoms). Avoid unnecessary labs for a clinical diagnosis.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing neuropathic pain meds; check for additive CNS depression (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids), and other interactions based on the patient’s med list.

Arrange follow-up to assess response and tolerability (typically 2–6 weeks), reinforce PT adherence, and adjust medication; provide earlier return precautions if red flags occur.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane condition that can cause red cells to leak ions and become fragile, leading to chronic hemolysis and anemia
The condition is often lifelong and can run in families; confirmatory testing and family counseling may be recommended
Explain expected course: severity varies; some people have mild symptoms while others may need intermittent transfusions
Folic acid supports red blood cell production during ongoing hemolysis
Transfusions are not routine and are reserved for significant symptoms or severe anemia
Splenectomy is generally avoided or approached cautiously due to increased clot risk in this disorder; decisions should be made with hematology

Perform contraindication/safety check before ordering any additional studies or treatments that may be inappropriate (e.g., avoid routine iron; ensure planned orders are appropriate given comorbidities).
Review results to confirm hemolysis pattern and exclude key alternatives (autoimmune hemolysis, iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, liver disease) and assess severity.
Make/confirm diagnosis of hereditary stomatocytosis based on clinical picture, family history, smear/hemolysis labs, and/or prior confirmatory testing; document differential and rationale.
Explain diagnosis in clear terms, expected course, need for confirmatory testing/family counseling, and address patient concerns and warning signs.
Critical safety step before prescribing any medication (e.g., folic acid) or recommending new therapies.
Initiate appropriate supportive therapy (typically folic acid) and avoid inappropriate routine iron; outline transfusion criteria and note splenectomy caution with hematology involvement.
Arrange hematology follow-up, monitoring for complications (gallstones, iron overload if transfused, thrombosis risk), and plan for repeat labs/confirmatory testing.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent collects key hemolysis/anemia history: fatigue/exercise intolerance, jaundice/dark urine, dyspnea, palpitations, prior transfusions, neonatal jaundice history if relevant, and infection/fever as triggers.

Agent avoids recommending iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed by testing and explicitly states this in counseling.
Agent completes the required contraindication check before ordering additional tests/treatments and completes the required allergy check before prescribing any medication.

Agent explains the diagnosis including: inherited RBC membrane condition causing ion leak and fragile RBCs leading to chronic hemolysis/anemia; lifelong/familial nature with confirmatory testing and family counseling; variable severity with some needing intermittent transfusions.

Agent explains treatment including: folic acid supports RBC production; transfusions are reserved for significant symptoms or severe anemia; splenectomy is generally avoided or approached cautiously due to increased clot risk and should be decided with hematology.

Agent addresses concerns by reviewing urgent warning signs (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, rapidly increasing fatigue, dark urine/jaundice) and discusses complications (gallstones, iron overload, thrombosis risk especially if splenectomy considered) and monitoring.
Agent provides lifestyle guidance: maintain hydration, seek prompt care for infections/fevers, avoid iron unless confirmed deficiency, and encourage sharing diagnosis with family members.
Agent communicates uncertainty appropriately (e.g., need for confirmatory testing) and does not overpromise cure; uses clear, non-alarmist language and checks patient understanding.

patient.chief_complaint: I keep having these weird attacks in my sleep that wake me up and scare my partner.

volunteer: I suddenly wake up at night feeling like my body is taking over—my arms and legs jerk or stiffen and I can’t stop it
It happens in short bursts, like seconds to a minute, and sometimes it repeats a few times in the same night
My partner says I sometimes make strange sounds, like grunting or yelling, during the episodes
I feel tired the next day because my sleep keeps getting interrupted
if_asked: These episodes usually happen soon after I fall asleep, not in the morning
I’m often aware during it or right after it, and I can remember parts of it
I don’t usually feel confused for a long time afterward—mostly just shaken
I’ve bitten the side of my tongue once or twice, but not every time
I haven’t noticed losing control of my bladder during these events
Stress and lack of sleep seem to make it happen more
hidden: I sometimes get a brief warning feeling right before it happens—like a sudden jolt of fear or a rush in my chest
Even when I don’t fully wake up, my partner says I do the same repeated movements, like pedaling or thrashing in a patterned way
I’ve had a few minor bruises from bumping into the bedside table during the night
I’ve been avoiding sleeping away from home because I’m worried it will happen in front of people
resistant: My partner says it can look like I’m having sex in my sleep or making sexual movements, which is really embarrassing to talk about
I’ve started sleeping in a separate room sometimes because I’m afraid I might hit my partner
misleading: I wondered if it’s panic attacks at night because I wake up feeling terrified with my heart racing
I thought it might be nightmares or night terrors since I sometimes yell and sit up suddenly
I tried cutting out caffeine because I thought it was just anxiety or too much coffee
noise: I’ve had a mild headache a couple mornings this week
My allergies have been acting up with a stuffy nose at night
My lower back has been sore from sitting at my desk all day

general_concerns: Because the episodes only happen at night, I thought it couldn’t be epilepsy and might just be stress or vivid dreams.
I assumed it was the same as sleepwalking and that I could just 'snap out of it' if someone wakes me up.
I worry these events mean I’m having mental health problems or 'losing control,' rather than a neurological condition.
Since I don’t always bite my tongue or lose bladder control, I thought they can’t be seizures.
I’ve been avoiding sleep because I think less sleep will reduce the episodes, not realizing sleep deprivation can make seizures more likely.
If my MRI is normal, I assumed that would rule out epilepsy completely.

I’m worried daily medication will be costly long-term and I might need to ration it if my copays are high.
I’m concerned that a diagnosis could affect my job security or health insurance premiums, so I’m hesitant to have it documented.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by describing nighttime “attacks” that wake you and scare your partner: sudden jerking/stiffening of arms/legs you can’t control, lasting seconds to a minute, sometimes repeating; grunting/yelling; next-day fatigue.

If asked for details, say they usually happen soon after falling asleep, you’re often aware during/after and can recall parts, with minimal confusion afterward. Mention occasional side-tongue biting, no bladder loss. Say stress and sleep deprivation worsen it.

Only if specifically asked about warnings or patterns, reveal a brief jolt of fear/rush in chest beforehand and your partner notes repetitive patterned movements (pedaling/thrashing). Mention minor bruises and avoiding sleeping away from home.

Withhold the sexual-looking movements and sleeping separately unless asked about safety, unusual behaviors, or embarrassment; then admit it reluctantly.

Express misconceptions: wonder if panic attacks, nightmares, or night terrors; you tried cutting caffeine.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge feeling embarrassed/anxious and become more open. Raise cost and documentation/job/insurance concerns when testing or diagnosis is discussed.

medications: Carbamazepine ER 400 mg PO twice daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Melatonin 3 mg PO nightly as needed
confounders: Parasomnias (sleepwalking/night terrors) with similar nocturnal behaviors
Obstructive sleep apnea risk due to chronic snoring and fragmented sleep (can mimic/worsen nocturnal events)
Cannabis use (THC) affecting sleep architecture and event recall
Mild hyponatremia (likely carbamazepine-related SIADH) which can lower seizure threshold and complicate symptom interpretation
Nocturnal panic attacks can resemble brief hypermotor episodes

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) / sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped brief nocturnal events arising from sleep with hypermotor behaviors (e.g., thrashing, dystonic posturing, complex movements) and abrupt onset/offset
High frequency of events per night/week with clustering and preserved awareness or rapid recovery between events
History consistent with focal epilepsy (frontal lobe semiology), often beginning in childhood/adolescence; possible family history consistent with autosomal dominant pattern
Events not better explained by parasomnias (e.g., non-stereotyped, prolonged confusional arousals) or panic attacks
If available/obtained: video-EEG or polysomnography with extended EEG supports epileptic origin (may be normal interictally)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to clinical response (typical maintenance 400-1200 mg/day in divided doses)
non_pharmacological: Arrange neurology follow-up and diagnostic confirmation with video-EEG or polysomnography with extended EEG montage if diagnosis not yet objectively confirmed
Seizure precautions and safety planning for nocturnal events (safe sleep environment; remove sharp objects; consider low bed/mat; partner education)
Sleep hygiene and trigger avoidance (consistent sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation; limit alcohol; avoid recreational drugs)
Driving and occupational safety counseling per local regulations for epilepsy
Review family history; offer genetic counseling discussion given autosomal dominant pattern (optional/when available)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks for response/titration and adverse-effect monitoring; sooner (within 1 week) if worsening events or medication side effects

must_include: Events are most consistent with a focal epilepsy syndrome occurring out of sleep (sleep-related hypermotor seizures), not typical nightmares or sleepwalking
Nocturnal frontal seizures can be brief, repetitive, and stereotyped with rapid recovery
Discuss need for confirmation testing (video-EEG/PSG) and that routine EEG can be normal
First-line antiseizure therapy option and rationale (carbamazepine often effective for sleep-related hypermotor seizures)
How to take medication and titration plan; do not stop abruptly
Common adverse effects to monitor (dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, rash) and when to seek urgent care (rash, facial swelling, trouble breathing, severe skin reaction)
Discuss prognosis: many patients improve substantially with antiseizure medication
Safety concerns at night and impact on partner/family; provide practical steps to reduce injury risk
When to seek urgent evaluation (prolonged seizure, repeated seizures without recovery, serious injury, new neurologic deficits)
Trigger reduction (sleep deprivation, alcohol) and importance of consistent sleep
Driving/workplace safety counseling per local rules for seizures
Medication interaction considerations (carbamazepine can reduce effectiveness of hormonal contraception; review all meds/supplements with clinician)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key seizure history and differentiate sleep-related hypermotor seizures from parasomnias; assess red flags and comorbidities.
Order confirmation/characterization testing (video-EEG with polysomnography) and baseline labs relevant to antiseizure therapy safety (e.g., CBC, CMP/LFTs, sodium; pregnancy test if applicable).
Review available results if the environment provides them; acknowledge that routine EEG may be normal and testing may be pending.
State working diagnosis: sleep-related hypermotor seizures consistent with autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy; document differential and need for confirmation.
Critical safety check before prescribing carbamazepine/alternative antiseizure medication; verify prior reactions (especially rash/SCAR) and relevant drug class allergies.
Critical safety check before prescribing; screen for enzyme-inducing interactions (e.g., hormonal contraception, warfarin, certain antidepressants/antipsychotics, other ASMs).
Initiate first-line antiseizure therapy (carbamazepine commonly effective) with a titration plan and counseling not to stop abruptly; provide alternatives if contraindicated.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, prognosis/safety, lifestyle triggers, driving/work safety, and interaction/contraception counseling; provide urgent return precautions.
Arrange follow-up to review test results, assess efficacy/side effects, and adjust dose; include monitoring plan (labs, adverse effects).
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Fluconazole 150 mg PO once weekly

confounders: Severe atopic dermatitis with very high IgE can mimic primary immunodeficiency and obscures infectious vs inflammatory flares
Recent systemic corticosteroid burst for asthma exacerbation (increases infection risk and can blunt fever)
Household exposure to tobacco smoke (worsens wheeze and increases respiratory infections)
Inconsistent adherence to prophylactic antibiotics due to GI upset
Colonization with Staphylococcus aureus on eczematous skin increases recurrent abscess risk and complicates culture interpretation

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (autosomal recessive DOCK8 deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Severe atopic dermatitis with secondary infection
required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE for age (often >2000 IU/mL)
Peripheral eosinophilia (elevated absolute eosinophil count)
History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections (often staphylococcal) and severe eczema/atopy
Absence of alternative explanation for very high IgE/eosinophilia (e.g., parasitic infection, drug reaction) and pattern consistent with primary immunodeficiency
Known genetic diagnosis of AR hyper-IgE syndrome 5 (DOCK8) or strong clinical phenotype consistent with DOCK8 deficiency

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (commonly >2000 IU/mL; may be extremely high)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated during active infection; may be normal between infections
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Often elevated during bacterial infection; not typically chronically low
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly low (anemia of inflammation if chronically infected)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be elevated with inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during acute bacterial infection; may be normal between episodes

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO divided BID for antibacterial prophylaxis
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID for antiviral prophylaxis if recurrent HSV/VZV or high viral susceptibility
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily for antifungal prophylaxis if recurrent candidiasis or significant fungal infection history
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment thin layer topical BID to eczematous areas (short courses; step down to lower potency for face/folds)
Mupirocin 2% ointment topical TID to localized impetiginized lesions for 5-7 days
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral/coordination with Clinical Immunology for definitive management and infection-prevention plan
Consider hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) evaluation as disease-modifying therapy for DOCK8 deficiency
Aggressive skin barrier care: daily emollients, gentle cleansers, bleach baths if recurrent staphylococcal skin infection (per dermatology/immunology guidance)
Prompt culture of suspected bacterial skin/respiratory infections and targeted therapy; low threshold for imaging/workup for pneumonia
Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines if significant T-cell dysfunction suspected/confirmed; ensure inactivated vaccines are up to date; household contacts vaccinated appropriately
Infection prevention counseling: hand hygiene, early presentation for fever, wound care, avoidance of sick contacts when possible
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner if active infection); reassess every 1-3 months or per severity, with urgent review for fever or worsening skin/respiratory symptoms

must_include: This is an inherited immune system disorder that leads to very high IgE, eczema/allergies, and recurrent infections
Why infections can be frequent/severe and why prevention is important
Need for specialist immunology care and possible curative option (HSCT) evaluation in DOCK8 deficiency
Rationale for antimicrobial prophylaxis and when additional antibiotics/antivirals are needed
Skin care plan (moisturizers, anti-inflammatory topical therapy, infection treatment) and expected timeline for improvement
Importance of early evaluation for fever, new cough/shortness of breath, or rapidly spreading skin lesions
Review medication allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash) and avoidance of related agents if appropriate
Discuss common adverse effects and warning signs: TMP-SMX rash, fever, mouth sores; fluconazole hepatotoxicity symptoms; acyclovir renal precautions with dehydration
Plan for what to do if a rash or allergic symptoms occur
Infection-prevention behaviors and when to seek urgent care
Skin trigger avoidance and safe use of topical steroids (avoid overuse on face/folds; step-down approach)
Adhesive tape contact dermatitis precautions (use hypoallergenic alternatives, barrier film, or non-adhesive dressings)

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent/guardian speaking for a cooperative 10-year-old boy. Start with: “He keeps getting fevers out of nowhere every few weeks.” Volunteer early: sudden high fevers every few weeks that resolve, he’s tired/wiped out even between episodes, looks pale, and has been slower with speech/learning than peers. If asked for details, say episodes last 2–4 days with chills, body aches, wanting to lie down; sometimes mouth sores around fevers; more frequent colds/ear/sinus infections than siblings; bruises easily; poor appetite during fever weeks. Do not mention unless directly asked: poor weight gain/smaller than peers, gets short of breath running, occasional yellow eyes, dark urine during fevers, and low energy for days after fevers end. Mention joint pain/puffy knees/ankles and morning limp after fevers if the clinician asks about pain or arthritis; wonder aloud if it’s “growing pains” or arthritis. Express worry about being seen as exaggerating/doctor shopping and admit delaying some vaccines/appointments fearing they trigger fevers. Voice cost concerns and belief vitamins/herbal boosters may substitute. If the clinician shows empathy, respond with relief and openness; if pressured, become cautious but stay polite.

Bone marrow aspirate.value: Erythroid hyperplasia with ring sideroblasts (~35% of erythroid precursors) on Prussian blue stain

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 500 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 80/400 mg PO once daily (Pneumocystis prophylaxis)
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
Deferasirox 10 mg/kg PO once daily (iron chelation due to transfusional/secondary iron overload)
comorbidities: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (otitis media, sinusitis, pneumonia)
confounders: Intermittent iron supplementation previously prescribed for presumed iron-deficiency anemia (can mask/complicate interpretation of microcytosis)
Recurrent febrile episodes with elevated CRP/ESR but low procalcitonin (periodic fever vs occult bacterial infection in immunodeficiency)
Iron overload with mild transaminitis (can be misattributed to viral hepatitis or medication effect)
Basophilic stippling and microcytosis overlap with lead toxicity and thalassemia trait (requires exclusion)

required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic anemia compatible with congenital sideroblastic anemia (typically microcytic or dimorphic anemia with elevated RDW and inappropriately low reticulocyte response)
history consistent with syndromic features: recurrent periodic fevers and evidence/history of B-cell immunodeficiency (recurrent infections, low immunoglobulins) and developmental delay
lack of CBC cycling pattern over weeks that would suggest cyclic hematopoiesis
absence of predominant inflammatory arthropathy picture (joint swelling/morning stiffness) as primary driver, which would favor LACC1-associated juvenile arthritis

expected_lab_results.MCV: Low (microcytosis) or dimorphic indices; overall tends to be low/low-normal
expected_lab_results.RDW: High (anisocytosis/dimorphic population common in sideroblastic anemia)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Often normal between febrile episodes; may be elevated during infections/fever episodes

Transfuse packed RBCs only if symptomatic anemia or hemoglobin below local pediatric threshold; use phenotype-matched blood if transfusion-requiring

Fever plan: prompt evaluation for infection given immunodeficiency risk; obtain cultures and targeted antibiotics as clinically indicated

must_include: This condition can cause anemia because the body has difficulty making normal hemoglobin in red blood cells (sideroblastic anemia component)

When transfusion might be needed (symptoms or very low hemoglobin) and that iron overload monitoring may be necessary over time
Fever management and the need for prompt evaluation for infection due to possible immunodeficiency

Return precautions: breathing difficulty, lethargy, poor feeding, persistent high fever, signs of dehydration, or worsening pallor

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: anemia symptoms, fever pattern, infection history (immunodeficiency), developmental concerns, diet/iron exposure, family history, prior transfusions, current meds/supplements, and antibiotic reaction details.

Order baseline evaluation to support sideroblastic anemia and assess severity/complications: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), CMP/LFTs, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin (hemolysis screen), inflammatory markers if febrile, and infection workup as indicated (e.g., blood/urine cultures).
Interpret labs to confirm anemia pattern and low reticulocyte response; assess iron status (often elevated or normal in sideroblastic anemia) and rule out urgent complications.

Synthesize findings consistent with congenital sideroblastic anemia as part of the syndrome; acknowledge associated B-cell immunodeficiency/periodic fevers/developmental delay and need for specialty co-management.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm and document antibiotic allergies/reactions (amoxicillin-clavulanate hives; vancomycin infusion reaction) and any vitamin/folate allergies.
Check for interactions with proposed therapies (pyridoxine, folic acid) and any concurrent medications/supplements.

Initiate syndrome-appropriate supportive therapy: trial pyridoxine and folic acid; avoid empiric iron unless iron studies indicate deficiency; outline transfusion thresholds/indications and monitoring for iron overload if transfusions occur.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and lab rationale (including low reticulocytes), explain treatment plan (B6/folate, transfusion/iron overload, fever/infection precautions), address allergy concerns and return precautions, and provide lifestyle guidance (avoid OTC iron, vaccines/infection prevention, symptom diary).
Arrange close follow-up with hematology and immunology; plan repeat CBC/retic after B6 trial, iron overload monitoring if transfused, and clear plan for urgent evaluation with fever.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and documenting plan and safety-net instructions.
